coredata.dc:description: Diseases characterized by amyloid aggregation, including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, and Type Ⅱ Diabetes mellitus, present a significant global public health challenge. The aberrant buildup and deposition of amyloid proteins w ithin the body are important markers and causative factors of these diseases. While early intervention strategies targeting amyloid aggregation can have some effect on the associated diseases, they are insufficient in preventing or reversing disease development. Carbon dots (CDs) have received a lot of attention in recent decades from a variety of scientific disciplines. In the context of amyloid aggregation disease, CDs have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in interacting with amyloid, inhibiting amyloid aggregation, reducing cytotoxicity, and overcoming physiological barriers in recent years. Latest developments in the field of amyloid aggregation disease involving CDs are introduced and discussed, highlighting the importance of their distinctive properties in inhibiting amyloid aggregation and reducing cytotoxicity. The discussion also addresses current challenges and future opportunities for clinical applications. Although the application of CDs in treating amyloid aggregation disease is still in its early stages, their ability to interact with amyloid effectively, inhibit aggregation effectively, and overcome physiological barriers signifies their potential as promising tools for future treatments in this domain.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diseases characterized by amyloid aggregation, including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, and Type Ⅱ Diabetes mellitus, present a significant global public health challenge. The aberrant buildup and deposition of amyloid proteins w ithin the body are important markers and causative factors of these diseases. While early intervention strategies targeting amyloid aggregation can have some effect on the associated diseases, they are insufficient in preventing or reversing disease development. Carbon dots (CDs) have received a lot of attention in recent decades from a variety of scientific disciplines. In the context of amyloid aggregation disease, CDs have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in interacting with amyloid, inhibiting amyloid aggregation, reducing cytotoxicity, and overcoming physiological barriers in recent years. Latest developments in the field of amyloid aggregation disease involving CDs are introduced and discussed, highlighting the importance of their distinctive properties in inhibiting amyloid aggregation and reducing cytotoxicity. The discussion also addresses current challenges and future opportunities for clinical applications. Although the application of CDs in treating amyloid aggregation disease is still in its early stages, their ability to interact with amyloid effectively, inhibit aggregation effectively, and overcome physiological barriers signifies their potential as promising tools for future treatments in this domain.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Protein needs to retain a relatively fixed structure for carrying out its specific function [1,2]. However, certain proteins may undergo conformational misfolding and subsequent aggregation, resulting in the loss of their normal function and contributing to associated diseases [3]. Amyloid aggregation refers to the process whereby amyloid proteins self-assemble into various forms of aggregates, such as oligomers, fibrils, and amyloid plaques [4]. The accumulation and deposition of these amyloid proteins in vivo serve as critical indicators and contributing factors to numerous neurodegenerative and metabolic disorders [5,6]. In recent years, the prevalence of amyloid aggregation illnesses has increased, and they are difficult to treat. Amyloid aggregation-related diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), Huntington's disease (HD), and Diabetes mellitus type Ⅱ (T2DM), are prevalent and difficult to manage [7,8]. Alzheimer's disease (AD), a prime example of amyloid aggregation disorders, is marked by a gradual and irreversible memory decline, cognitive function deficiency, and communication skills disability [9–11]. The commonly accepted pathogenic mechanism of AD involves self-assembling β-amyloid (Aβ) peptides and subsequent depositing them in vivo [12,13]. An increase in Aβ levels, due to either defective clearance or excessive production, leads to the formation of toxic oligomers and fibrillar aggregates from Aβ monomers [14]. Moreover, oligomers and fibrillar aggregates contribute to oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction in neurons. Therefore, inhibiting Aβ aggregation presents potential treatment methods for AD [15]. Similarly, the progressive neurodegenerative disease Parkinson's disease (PD) is defined by the self-assembly of non-functional amyloid structures from functioning Alpha-Synuclein (α-Syn) [16]. There is currently no effective treatment or precaution for this illness [16,17]. Nonetheless, inhibition of α-Syn aggregation is considered a promising therapeutic strategy for PD. Meanwhile, HD and T2DM are also closely associated with amyloid aggregation. Huntingtin's disease is characterized by intracellular aggregation of the mutant huntingtin, whose pathological importance has been subject to long-standing controversy [18]. Cryo-electron tomography was used by Bauerlein et al. to reveal the fibrillary structure of huntingtin aggregates in situ. Their results suggest that the endoplasmic reticulum interacts with huntingtin fibrils, resulting in morphological and dynamic abnormalities [19]. Amyloid deposits accumulate between islet capillaries and beta cells in type 2 diabetes (T2DM) due to the aggregation of human islet amyloid polypeptide (hIAPP), which is harmful to beta cells [20].

As was previously indicated, the misfolding and aberrant aggregation of the corresponding amyloid proteins is one of the main pathogenic causes for AD, PD, HD, and T2DM [84]. Therefore, inhibiting the misfolding and abnormal aggregation of these amyloid proteins presents a potential therapeutic approach for treating amyloid aggregation diseases. Recent studies have emphasized the application of natural drugs, organic compounds, and nanomaterials, particularly nanoparticles, in the treatment of amyloid aggregation diseases. These materials are advantageous due to their tissue permeability, bioimaging capabilities, therapeutic functions, and unique physicochemical properties [85–90]. Among these, carbon dots (CDs) represent a broad category encompassing nanoscale photoluminescent carbon materials, which are a growing subclass of nanoparticles characterized by quasi-spherical or amorphous structures [91,92]. This category includes carbon nanodots (CNDs), carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [93]. In recent years, the design of CDs with various functions and their extensive application in amyloid aggregation diseases have become hot topics in related research fields [94,95]. The emergence of nanotechnology has facilitated the development of various kinds of pure CDs, modified CDs, and drug-conjugated CDs (Fig. 1 a), which are promising therapeutic agents. These CDs offer distinct advantages, including photo-responsive therapy, high drug-loading capacity, and effective tissue penetration [96]. Notably, the application of photo-responsive CDs for controlling amyloid aggregation has garnered interest owing to their photo-sensitive characteristics. Research indicates that light-based treatments can significantly improve the effectiveness of reducing cytotoxicity induced by amyloid aggregation [53,80,97]. Fig. 1b illustrates the number of research publications in recent years, highlighting the use of CDs in amyloid aggregation diseases. Carbon dots can traverse the blood-brain barrier in several different ways because they are a zero-dimensionality substance. The passive diffusion, transporter-mediated endocytosis, receptor-mediated transport, and other mechanisms are included in this category. A further symptom of Alzheimer's disease is the disruption of the blood-brain barrier, which makes it possible for tiny carbon dots to enter the brain.

Based on the backdrop of aggregation disease and the benefits of CDs in treating aggregation disease. This review highlights the application of CDs (including CNDs, CPDs, CQDs, GQDs and other CDs related nanomaterials) in the treatment of aggregation diseases (including AD, PD, HD, T2DM and other aggregation diseases) in recent years. Meanwhile, the interaction mechanism between CDs and amyloid, as well as the benefits of in vivo and ex vivo applications, are also thoroughly investigated. Furthermore, it is concluded that combining CDs with innovative therapeutic approaches is a promising new approach to treating aggregation diseases. Finally, the future challenges and opportunities for the practical application of CDs in the treatment of aggregation diseases are discussed.

GQDs are carbon-based nanoparticles with remarkable chemical, biological, and physical capabilities. They are generally less than 10 nm. These characteristics make them extremely useful in a wide range of nanomedicine applications [98]. Studies have shown that GQDs don't seem to be harmful in either in vitro or in vivo setting. Interestingly, GQDs have been shown to be able to pass the blood-brain barrier (BBB) because of their tiny size and capacity for transcytosis [99,100]. Currently, pure GQDs, modified GQDs, and drug-conjugated GQDs have been shown to effectively inhibit the aggregation of various types of amyloid proteins and mitigate the cytotoxic effects of amyloid aggregates [21,30]. As a result, research has extensively explored various GQDs-based nanomaterials for their ability to influence amyloid assembly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pure GQDs for inhibiting amyloid aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: Preventing the aggregation of Aβ peptides is critical in combating Alzheimer's disease (AD). Thus, inhibiting Aβ peptide aggregation has emerged as a pivotal strategy in the treatment of AD [101]. GQDs were created by Liu et al. as effective, low-cytotoxicity inhibitors, and they were used to stop Aβ peptides from aggregating. The results of ThT fluorescence assay showed that as the surface negative charge reduced, GQDs' ability to suppress Aβ peptide aggregation improved noticeably. Treating the cells with Aβ peptides in the presence of GQDs greatly increased cell survival, raising it to almost 80 % from the low cell viability of 41 % seen with Aβ1-42 peptides alone [21]. Similarly, Tang et al. conducted a comprehensive analysis of the cytotoxicity of Aβ species and GQDs/Aβ species from the cell membrane level [22]. The impact of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the rate and structures of amyloid-beta (Aβ) aggregation was initially investigated using a ThT fluorescence kinetic test and transmission electron microscopy (Fig. 2 a). They investigated how GQDs affect the way that Aβ interacts with the cell membrane in three different forms: fibrils, oligomers, and monomers. Their investigation, which measured the in vitro alterations in membrane fluidity brought about by Aβ species and GQDs, showed that GQDs were successful in lessening the disruption of the membrane that Aβ oligomers created. Furthermore, they found that GQDs possess the potential to mitigate the toxicity of Aβ oligomers by reducing actin reorganization and preventing cell death. Detecting amyloid is equally crucial for inhibiting their aggregation to prevent amyloid formation. Moreover, Tak et al. synthesized GQDs using one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis from clitoria ternatea flowers for treating Alzheimer's disease [23]. The findings demonstrated that GQDs effectively inhibit AchE activity, elevate glutathione and protein levels, and reduce lipid peroxide and nitric oxide levels. Furthermore, results from the Water Morris Maze Assay clearly indicate that the GQDs reduce transfer latency to reach the hidden platform more effectively than a pure donepezil drug. GQDs serve not only as inhibitors of amyloid aggregation but also as detectors of amyloid aggregation. Huang et al. discovered that GQDs can function as fluorescent sensors to measure Aβ concentrations [24]. Other studies have shown that GQDs exhibit similar Aβ aggregation dynamics as ThT, validating the feasibility of using GQDs for amyloid detection. It is clear from the aforementioned research that GQDs interact with the amyloid by surface interface interactions (such as electrostatic, hydrophobic, and other strong interactions) to prevent the amyloid aggregation process. By preventing the production of different amyloid aggregates, GQDs can simultaneously produce therapeutic effects at the cellular and animal levels. Thus, GQDs show a lot of promise as medications for illnesses linked to amyloid accumulation.

Interestingly, GQDs have shown capability not only to suppress the fibrillation of Aβ but also to effectively inhibit the aggregation of other amyloids. For instance, fibrillar deposits of the hIAPP are implicated as a primary cause of Type II diabetes mellitus. GQDs have been shown by Wang et al. to be effective as strong inhibitors of IAPP aggregation and toxicity in vivo. The process of amyloid aggregation is significantly influenced by the β-sheet composition present in them. They found that IAPP pre-incubated with GQDs resulted in a significant reduction in β-sheet content, and that the reduction was linked with higher concentrations of GQDs [25]. Additionally, Faridi et al. identified 29 proteins that exhibited significant dysregulation in the presence of IAPP species, particularly IAPP oligomers, predominantly comprising nucleotide-binding proteins [26]. Subsequently, the authors showed that GQDs effectively modulate aberrant protein expression in β-cell lines induced by IAPP. The interaction between GQDs and IAPPo or IAPPf is quantitatively determined, as seen in Fig. 2b. Despite IAPP oligomers causing significant damage to β-cells, GQDs mitigate the cell toxicity induced by these oligomers through complex interactions between IAPP and GQDs.

Extensive research indicates a strong correlation between the pathogenesis of Parkinson's disease and the accumulation and spread of α-synuclein (α-syn) aggregates in the midbrain. Kim et al. discovered that GQDs can inhibit the fibrillization of α-syn by interacting with mature α-syn fibrils (Fig. 2c) [27]. According to Kim et al., GQDs can lessen synaptic loss and neuronal death, lessen the development of Lewy bodies and neurites, enhance mitochondrial function, and stop the spread of α-syn disease from one neuron to another caused by preexisting α-syn fibrils. In a mouse model with preformed fibrils, the researchers demonstrated that GQDs could fully penetrate the brain. For a period of six months, biweekly intraperitoneal injections offered significant defense against toxicity caused by produced fibrils in the substantia nigra's dopaminergic neurons. Consequently, mice treated with GQDs exhibited fewer motor deficits compared to untreated mice and displayed no associated toxicity with GQD treatment. Furthermore, Ghaeidamini et al. discovered that GQDs can sequester α-syn monomers, thereby reducing the aggregation rate of α-syn [28]. This interaction prevents the primary nucleation and elongation of α-syn. However, the inhibitory effect of GQDs on aggregation is mainly due to the adsorption of aggregated species and the reduction of secondary processes, meaning it cannot completely prevent aggregation. In summary, GQDs, like Aβ, inhibit α-syn, preventing the formation of amyloid aggregates by reducing the creation of primary nucleation and ultimately the elongation of amyloid aggregates.

The RNA-binding protein fused in sarcoma (FUS), which is linked to amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), has notable liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS) properties and is essential for RNA synthesis, stress granule building, and DNA repair [102]. Research has shown that mutations in FUS associated with ALS can disrupt its transport between the nucleus and cytoplasm, hinder its dynamic LLPS assembly in SGs, and lead to pathological aggregation in the cytoplasm, contributing to AL [103]. The ability of GQDs to bind directly to the protein FUS linked to SGs has been shown by Zhang et al. to block and reverse the LLPS of the protein and stop its aberrant phase transition. Furthermore, FUS amyloid aggregation was strongly inhibited and produced FUS fibrils were dispersed by GQDs.

Biofilms formed by bacteria are integral components of Earth's ecosystem, posing numerous ecological, technological, and health challenges. Biofilms contain many biological macromolecules such as proteins, polysaccharides, DNA, RNA, peptidoglycan, peptides, and phospholipids [104]. These biological macromolecules form an extracellular matrix that serves a protective function for bacteria. In addition, proteins and peptides within biofilms can self-assemble into dense amyloid aggregates, which poses challenges for antibiotics in penetrating biofilms [105]. Therefore, it is crucial for antibacterial strategies to inhibit the formation of amyloid aggregates in biofilms. Wang et al. observed that GQDs efficiently disrupt the self-formation of amyloid fibrils and disperse mature biofilms enriched with amyloids in Staphylococcus aureus [71]. In transmission electron microscopy (TEM) experiments, amyloid fibrils and the surrounding extracellular matrix extracted from the biofilm cultures were observed. GQDs profoundly changed the extracellular matrix's and amyloid fibrils' shape and integrity at a 50 μg mL−1 dosage. Peptone-NaCl-glucose (PNG) biofilm's isolated amyloid fibrils are primarily made up of PSMs, which are tiny α-helical amphipathic peptides. PSMα1 is one of these peptides that can form fibrils that resemble amyloid. To delve deeper into the effects of GQDs, commercially available synthetic PSMα1 was utilized in their investigation. They found that the aggregation process of PSMα1 was effectively inhibited by GQDs in a concentration-dependent manner, regardless of whether GQDs were cultured with amyloid for 4 or 9 days. By analyzing the secondary structure of the amyloid fibrils using Circular dichroism (CD) spectrometry, they found that GQDs could induce the breakdown of the composite structure of amyloid fibrils of the extracted biofilm extracellular matrix within this specific duration.

In conclusion, cellular experiments have demonstrated that GQDs possess cell-protective qualities and considerably lessen Aβ protein aggregation in Alzheimer's disease patients. Moreover, GQDs have demonstrated the ability to prevent hIAPP and other amyloid proteins from aggregating. Studies on Parkinson's disease have shown that GQDs and α-synuclein interact, and effectively inhibiting fibrillation and enhance nerve function. Additionally, GQDs have shown promise in disrupting amyloid aggregates in bacterial biofilms, suggesting potential new strategies for antimicrobial development. These findings offer novel insights into the pathogenesis of related diseases and suggest new directions for treating and preventing amyloid diseases (see Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modified GQDs for inhibiting amyloid aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: Although pure GQDs show excellent performance in inhibiting amyloid aggregation, their effectiveness is still not ideal. To improve its overall physicochemical properties and achieve an ideal inhibition performance, chemically-functionalized GQDs have been developed and widely applied in the biomedical field [106]. Generally, the methods for functionalizing GQDs to resist amyloid aggregation include drug loading, immobilizing functional molecules, and doping with functional components (see Table 2). First and foremost, the distinct structure of GQDs allows them to effectively carry aromatic compounds, making them highly suitable for small-molecule drug delivery. For example, Zhao et al. reviewed GQDs' applications in drug delivery, introducing that GQDs can bind with a variety of aromatic bimolecular (such as 5-fluorouracil and Adriamycin) through the π–π stacking interaction and/or electrostatic binding [107]. In addition, it is also critical to select drug with anti-amyloid aggregation properties. Xiao et al. demonstrated this by using GQDs as carriers to conjugate neuroprotective drugs, specifically glycine-proline-glutamate, thereby creating a GQDs-drug delivery system [30]. This GQDs-drug delivery system could inhibit the aggregation of Aβ fibrils and promote the generation of neuronal precursor cells and neurons. Moreover, related data indicated that this system could enhance learning and memory capacity and increase the number of dendritic spines in APP/PS1 transgenic mice. Additionally, the GQDs-drug delivery system reduced several pro-inflammatory cytokines while increasing corresponding anti-inflammatory cytokines compared to the control group. GQDs play a vital role in the treatments of nanosystems, including the delivery of neuroprotective drugs, brain enrichment, and enhanced treatment efficiency. Synergistic inhibition, based on drugs and GQDs, represents a new high-efficiency inhibitory strategy. Liu et al. designed a highly efficient and low-cytotoxic Aβ aggregation inhibitor (GQD-T) by integrating GQDs and tramiprosate [31]. They found that GQD-T effectively inhibits Aβ peptide aggregation and mitigates Aβ-induced cytotoxicity, attributed to the synergistic interaction between GQDs and tramiprosate (Fig. 3 a). Moreover, GQD-T exhibits low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Fluorine-functionalized GQDs have also been proven effective against amyloid aggregation. Yousaf et al. used the microwave-assisted hydrothermal process to create a novel molecule known as fluorine-functionalized GQDs (FGQDs). The investigation proved that FGQDs might effectively prevent amyloid aggregation (Fig. 3b) [32]. They found that FGQDs could inhibit the peptide's conformational shift from its native structure to β-sheets, thereby mitigating the cytotoxic effects on INS-1 cells induced by hIAPP in a manner dependent on dosage. According to the literature reviewed above, GQDs not only serve a therapeutic role as amyloid aggregation inhibitors, but they also work with loaded drug molecules to perform a therapeutic effect and boost medication efficacy. In vivo investigations have validated GQDs' BBB breakthrough performance. Based on these considerations, the combination of GQDs with AD medicinal medicines has demonstrated many great features and created surprising results. This can be a useful reference while designing nanomedicines.

For the inhibition and detection of amyloid aggregation, especially for the real-time, in-situ, quantitative and rapid detection of amyloid aggregation process, as well as nanomedicine for the integrated diagnosis and treatment of amyloid aggregation diseases, have been widely developed [6,108]. Yousaf et al. designed a multimodal probe utilizing bovine-serum-albumin-capped fluorine functionalized graphene quantum dots (BSA@FGQDs) for detecting monomers and oligomers of amyloid peptides. BSA@FGQDs are equipped for both specified fluorescence imaging and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [33]. This research demonstrates that BSA@FGQDs are capable of in vivo monitoring of human islet amyloid polypeptide (hIAPP), insulin, and Aβ42 monomers and oligomers throughout fibrillogenesis (Fig. 3c). Moreover, they could use 19F MRI with contrast enhancement in vivo to find amyloid plaques in the brains of living mice. Furthermore, as compared to traditional thioflavin dye, BSA@FGQDs show signals ten times stronger in the detection of amyloid intermediates and fibrils. Heteroatom-doped GQDs have proven to be powerful inhibitors of amyloid aggregation due to their unique chemical, electronic and optical properties. To further explore the impact of heteroatom doping on protein aggregation, Zeng et al. investigated the impact of as-prepared N-GQDs on HEWL aggregation using HEWL as a model amyloidogenic protein [34]. The findings showed that N-GQDs effectively inhibited HEWL fibrillation under conditions of high temperature and low pH in a manner dependent on dosage. In order to create a composite Ag@SiO2@N, S-GQDs nanocomp osite, Yu et al. used N, S-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs) to self-assemble on the surface of Ag@SiO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 3d) [35]. They further utilized this nanocomposite to modify Aβ1-42, resulting in a probe with enhanced photoluminescence properties (Ag@SiO2@N, S-GQDs-Aβ1-42). Their study revealed a competitive reaction between Aβ1-42 and Ag@SiO2@N, S-GQDs-Aβ1-42 towards anti-Aβ1-42 immobilized on the ELISA plate, facilitated by specific antigen-antibody interaction. The experimental findings highlighted that the immunoassay (Ag@SiO2@N, S-GQDs-Aβ1-42) exhibited robust analytical performance, offering a novel approach for clinical determining Aβ1-42 levels. Interestingly, the functionalized versions of GQDs mentioned above incorporate their photoluminescence properties. Functionalized modified GQDs can also demonstrate outstanding detection capabilities of amyloid monomers, oligomers, and other amyloid aggregates with appropriate molecular modification and elemental doping, making them a useful detection tool for the early diagnosis of AD.

Chemical modification of GQDs enhances their potential for biomedical applications, especially in neuroprotective drug delivery and inhibition of amyloid aggregation. Methods such as drug loading onto GQDs, immobilizing functional molecules, and doping functional components are employed for this purpose. These modifications can effectively inhibit amyloid aggregation, offering therapeutic benefits in neuroprotection and improving learning and memory. In addition, nitrogen and sulfur-doped GQDs, as well as fluorinated GQDs, demonstrate enhanced efficacy in inhibiting amyloid aggregation.

CQDs are a kind of zero-dimensional carbon-dominated nanomaterial. They are distinct from spherical, tubular, and sheet-like structures, and are considered part of the family of graphene-based nanomaterials [109]. CQDs, with a size smaller than 10 nm, possess a carbon core embedded with oxygen and nitrogen groups or polymer chains on the outer layer [110]. These nanomaterials have been widely employed in bioimaging, drug administration, clinical diagnosis, and therapy due to their exceptional features, including excellent photoluminescence capabilities, high biocompatibility, and versatile surface functionalization, and the availability of abundant and cost-effective raw ingredients [111,112].

CQDs offer great properties such as affordability, ease of synthesis, high biocompatibility, and photoluminescence capabilities [113]. There are several ways to modify their absorption and emission spectra, including changing the type of precursor, the synthesis method, the degree of carbonization, the surface state, and element doping [114]. Additionally, functional groups including carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups are prevalent and easily modifiable in CQDs. Through hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, hydrophobic contacts, and electrostatics, these functional groups allow interactions with amyloid and associated aggregates [109].

CQDs derived from biomass macromolecules exhibit minimal cytotoxicity, are cost-effective, and possess exceptional optical characteristics [115,116]. One of the most efficient strategies to avoid resource wastage and promote sustainable technological advancements is to convert low-value biomass into high-value carbon material CQDs [117]. Hydrothermal carbonization, microwave carbonization, and pyrolysis carbonization are three commonly used processes for producing CQDs from biomass. Using NaOH/H2O2 treatment, Jing et al. demonstrated a simple, low-cost, and extremely effective method for mass-producing CQDs from carbon obtained from biomass, such as hydrochar and carbonized biomass [118]. Ren et al. used a low-cost, eco-friendly one-step hydrothermal technique to turn the biomass waste of peach leaves into CQDs [119]. The CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence properties with a quantum yield of 7.71 % even without any passivation, and they demonstrate low biotoxicity. Fang et al. highlighted the feasibility and benefits of using biomass waste as a carbon source for carbon point synthesis, as well as the technique and use of biomass waste for carbon point synthesis [120]. Koppel et al. synthesized CQDs using lignite and discovered that lipopolysaccharide (LPS) promoted amyloid fibrosis [121]. The researchers examined the process of aggregation of IAPP or Aβ in the presence of LPS and CQDs, as seen in Fig. 4 a. LPS specifically demonstrated preferential binding to the C terminus of Aβ and the N terminus of IAPP, as well as the hydrophobic core of IAPP. On the other hand, CQDs demonstrated a preference for interacting with the N terminus of Aβ and the amyloidogenic and C-terminal areas at the IAPP. To confirm the inhibitory effects of CQDs, they used an embryonic zebrafish model exposed to both the peptide and LPS. It was demonstrated that the nanomaterials restored impaired embryo hatching and suppressed reactive oxygen species generation in the organism. This work indicates a significant synergistic effect of LPS and amyloid peptides in inducing toxicity, suggesting that CQDs could potentially target the pathologies of T2D and AD. Wu et al. conducted a review on the applications of biomass CQDs in energy storage, renewable energy, drug delivery, biosensing, and bioimaging [122]. Zhao et al. synthesized a variety of photoluminescent CDs using biomass as precursors, demonstrating their utiligy in bacterial imaging, while Tariq reported on the figs-derived CDs showing promising antibacterial properties [123,124]. The excellent fluorescence performance of biomass macromolecular derived CQDs are advantageous for the diagnosis and treatment of AD. Recent developments in the synthesis procedure, formation mechanisms, structural features, optical qualities, and uses of lignocellulose-based CDs were reviewed by Gan et al. [125]. They also proposed future research directions aimed at improving the synthesis process of CDs from lignocellulosic biomass to further improve their properties. Kumar et al. introduced a straightforward and environmentally friendly method to produce highly luminescent carbon spots using camel milk (CM) [126]. The CM-derived CDs exhibited anti-alpha-synuclein amyloid activity, with an average hydrodynamic diameter of 3–15 nm and significant photoluminescence (PL) in the blue region (Fig. 4b). However, the research to date indicates that CQDs derived from biomass macromolecules are only marginally linked to amyloid-related aggregation illnesses. Nonetheless, Biomass macromolecular derived-CQDs perform exceptionally well in terms of biocompatibility and rich surface functional groups, which has significant application potential for studying amyloid-related aggregation diseases and merits further investigation by additional researchers. In addition, due to the complex composition of biomass macromolecular derived-CQDs, further exploration is needed in terms of mechanism of action before they can be better applied to amyloid-related diseases.

Wu et al. reviewed the applications of biomass CQDs in energy storage, renewable energy, drug delivery, biosensing and bioimaging [127]. They emphasized that future synthesis of CQDs will likely prioritize green technologies, such as using biomass as a carbon source. Enhancing the yield of biomass-derived CQDs is essential for their viability in practical applications. Optimizing efficient, large-scale production methods is critical for producing high-quality CDs with small, uniform sizes and high quantum yields. Uniform sizes ensure consistent emission properties within a narrow range, while a high quantum yield increases the brightness of the CQDs. Smaller sizes enhance the functionalized surface area, expanding their potential use, particularly in human-related applications. These properties are especially advantageous for biological imaging.

Small molecule-derived CQDs can be synthesized from a wide range of sources, with suitable precursors chosen based on existing knowledge. The purification process for these CQDs is simpler than that of biomass-derived CDs. Additionally, small molecule-derived CQDs possess abundant surface groups, making them easier to modify [128]. Their preparation method employs bottom-up methods, which involve synthesizing of CQDs from smaller carbon units such as organic molecules.

In 2009, Peng et al. reported a simple aqueous method for preparing luminescent carbon source spots, using carbohydrates as starting materials [129]. These spots, termed carbon source spots due to their oxygen content, were intended for cell imaging applications. Recently, the development of small molecule-derived CQDs has progressed significantly, enhancing their performance in bioimaging and demonstrating considerable advantages in inhibiting amyloid aggregation. Guerrero et al. produced CQDs with sodium citrate as a precursor. They discovered that these CQDs might interfere with several points along the fibril formation process, inhibiting the conversion of monomeric and oligomeric HEWL intermediates into complete fibrils [36]. Prabhu obtained similar results by creating CQDs by spices [37,38]. Ahlawat et al. synthesized three distinct CQDs utilizing carbon-containing precursors such as sodium citrate, phenylboronic acid, and 4-aminobenylboronic acid [39]. While all three types of CQDs could prevent the conversion of soluble monomers of egg white lysozyme into mature fibrils, those derived from sodium citrate and 4-aminobenoboric acid also exhibited the ability to scavenge reactive oxygen species when exposed to a variety of free radical producers. Furthermore, CQDs derived from sodium citrate and 5-aminobenzoboronic acid protected neurons from rotenone-induced cell death. Liu et al. developed and produced nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) using 1,2,3-benzenetricarboxylic acid and o-phenylenediamine precursors. These N-CDs act as an Aβ-targeted probe, inhibiting Aβ42 self-assembly in vitro [40]. According to Voronova et al., complex protein aggregates that are hazardous to β-cells are formed when hIAPP and Aβ42 interact. They discovered that the creation of IAPP β42 heterocomplexes and single amyloid aggregation are both successfully inhibited by positively charged CQDs (CQD-pos) [41]. Lim et al. investigated the inhibitory effects of curcumin quantum dots on Aβ aggregation and Tau hyperphosphorylation. They found that Cur-CQDs pyrolyzed at 210 °C showed the most significant effect. This enhanced performance was likely due to the abundant anisole and guaiacol moieties present on the surface of the Cur-CQDs [42]. Han et al. prepared C-Dots using carbon powder in acidic solution and discovered that C-Dots effectively inhibited Aβ aggregation, targeted upstream secretases, and reduced the cytotoxicity of Aβ fibrils [43]. Yang et al. suggested a novel kind of chiral CQDs produced from cysteine, which specifically targets plasmin, a fundamental endogenous protease involved in fibrinolysis in humans. These CQDs may operate as regulators of fibrinolytic activity for plasmin, causing it to break down hIAPP into non-toxic polypeptides or smaller amino acid fragments. This process helps to reduce the harmful effects of hIAPP's fibrotic amyloid-induced cytotoxicity. Results demonstrate that chiral CQDs have contrasting impacts on plasmin activity. The L-CQDs enhance plasmin activity, resulting in a 23.2 % increase in the cleavage of hIAPP. This, in turn, protects β-cells from the harmful effects of amyloid. On the other hand, the resulting D-CQDs effectively suppress proteolysis, reducing plasmin activity by 31.5 % using the same reaction conditions (Fig. 4c) [44]. Li et al. used the pulsed laser ablation (PLA) method to create ultra-small CQDs (C-QDs) with uniform sizes [45]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), thioflavin T, and circular dichroism (CD) analyses confirmed that these C-QDs attenuated the aggregation of Aβ42. As can be seen from the above research, small molecule-derived CQDs are used more frequently in amyloid aggregation diseases than biological macromolecule-derived CQDs. These small molecule-derived CQDs have high product purity, tunable structures, and the ability to design the structure of CQDs beforehand, which is very useful for customizing amyloid aggregation inhibitors (see Table 3).

As research progresses, scientists have discovered that CQDs not only inhibit amyloid aggregation but also exhibit significant effects on oxidative stress, bacterial infections, and other pathological conditions in amyloid diseases. Our group created a high-performance nanomaterial called gCDs-E by self-assembling glycosylated CDs (gCDs) with epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG) [46]. gCDs-E targets not only Aβ aggregation but also Candida albicans infections and can readily penetrate the blood-brain barrier (Fig. 4d). Henriquez et al. investigated the neuroprotective effects of citric acid-derived CQDs (Cit-CQDs) on paraquat-treated human neuroblastoma-derived SHSY5Y cell lines and paraquat-exposed nematodes (Caenorhabditis elegans) [47]. Their findings indicated that Cit-CQDs could scavenge free radicals and reduce the level of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) induced by paraquat in SH-SY5Y cells. Cit-CQDs effectively protected cell lines and organisms from neuronal damage and death associated with poisoning. Pokharel et al. suggested that CNDs demonstrate radical scavenging capabilities, specifically targeting H2O2 and NO with Aβ proteins [130]. Li et al. utilized the fluorescent property of Congo Red (CR) that responds with red fluorescence when in contact with protein aggregates, leading them to develop a series of CR-derived CDs using CR itself as the carbon source [48]. To enhance the anti-aggregation and antioxidant properties of these CR-derived CDs, they deliberately reacted CR with citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), or ascorbic acid (AA). The resulting CDs could turn on red fluorescence during protein aggregate imaging, inhibiting protein aggregation, and scavenging free radicals. Among the CDs studied, CA-CDs, derived using citric acid as the carbon source, exhibited superior integration of these functionalities compared to EDA-CDs and AA-CDs (Fig. 4E). Particularly noteworthy was their excellent cytoprotective effect against toxic Aβ42 species, suggesting significant potential for future research in the diagnosis and treatment of AD. This study represents a novel approach in developing multifunctional CDs for research into protein-related diseases. Therefore, due to the careful selection of precursors and the potent physicochemical characteristics of CQDs, it has been discovered that CDs, particularly CQDs, not only prevent amyloid aggregation but also control other pathogenic factors linked to amyloid aggregation diseases. This discovery is based on a thorough analysis of the structure of CQDs and an understanding of the pathophysiology of related diseases.

Several researchers have delved deeper into understanding its mechanisms and have achieved significant breakthroughs. Wei et al. designed a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method to create red-emitting CDs derived from quercetin (Que) and p-phenylene diamine (p-PD) [49]. These CDs, denoted as R-CD-75, inherit structural and functional groups from their precursors Que and p-PD, including aromatic structures, phenolic hydroxyl groups, and amino groups. They interact with Aβ through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, hydrophobic interaction, and π-π stacking, enabling them to serve as effective diagnostic and therapeutic tools for both diagnosis and therapy in AD. Zhou et al. used O-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors to synthesis amphiphilic yellow luminous Y-CDs via an ultrasound-mediated approach [50]. These Y-CDs exhibited uniform particle sizes and demonstrated the ability to penetrate the blood-brain barrier through the spinal cord, highlighting their passive diffusion mechanism. Moreover, Y-CDs showed promising capabilities in crossing both the blood-brain barrier and cell membranes, effectively inhibiting the overexpression of APP and Aβ. Bloch et al. found that CDs produced from tyrosine decreased hIAPP fibrillation, reduced hIAPP-induced cell toxicity, and prevented membrane disruption caused by the peptide [51]. This inhibitory effect was attributed to the presence of aromatic residues abundantly present on the surface of the CDs. Malishev et al. employed CDs synthesized from either l-lysine or d-lysine to influence the aggregation and cytotoxicity of amyloid Aβ42 [52]. L-Lys-CDs notably altered the secondary structure and fibrils morphology of Aβ42, while also inhibiting the cytotoxic effects and membrane interactions of Aβ42 (Fig. 4f). In another approach, Ye et al. coated macrophage membranes on N-CNDs to synthesize CQD-RAW, which penetrated the BBB under NIR light exposure [53]. CQD-RAW effectively captured excess Cu2+ ions, regulating the metal ion homeostasis. Wang et al. engineered a versatile diagnostic nanocomplex, HSA-BFP@CDs, by combining human serum albumin (HSA) with blue fluorescent protein (BFP) and CDs [54]. HSA-BFP@CDs exhibited a fluorescence "off-on" response at 700 nm upon interacting with Aβ aggregates. Importantly, HSA-BFP@CDs effectively inhibited Aβ aggregation. Additionally, this nanocomplex demonstrated the ability to neutralize various ROS, including hydroxyl radicals, superoxide radicals, hydrogen peroxide, and Aβ-Cu2+-induced ROS, thereby mitigating oxidative damage within cells. Wang et al. discovered that CDs, produced from citric acid and glutathione in a one-step hydrothermal technique, act as a powerful intracellular ROS scavenger, decreasing lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced inflammation in macrophages [55]. These CDs possess broad-spectrum antioxidant characteristics, showing over 51.6 % higher overall antioxidant activity compared to the precursor glutathione at the same mass concentration. In addition to their antioxidant properties, the study investigated CD's efficiency in mitigating LPS-induced macrophage inflammation. It was shown that CDs could successfully remove up to 98 % of intracellular ROS while decreasing the expression of the inflammatory cytokine IL12. These findings highlight CDs' potential as very effective intracellular ROS scavengers and their prospective use in the treatment of oxidative stress-related diseases. Yan et al. synthesized a series of CDs with varying structures were synthesized by adjusting the molar ratio of citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA). With increasing EDA content, CDs exhibited a rise in the number of amino functional groups while experiencing a reduction in carboxyl groups. CDs possessing more amino functional groups effectively induced alterations in the initial conformation of Aβ42, leading to a significant inhibition of Aβ42 aggregation [131].

To obtain carbon points with specific properties, selecting the appropriate precursor and employing the suitable preparation method is crucial. Further exploration of the CDs’ structure is essential to facilitate the acquisition of CDs with certain properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CPDs, CNDs and other types of CDs for inhibiting amyloid aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: CNDs are nanoparticles with an amorphous and quasi-spherical structure. They do not have quantum confinement, but they do have excited states that come from molecular-like species [132]. Generally, CNDs are larger than CQDs, typically ranging from 5 to 100 nm. In contrast to a pure carbon framework, CPDs have a polymer/carbon hybrid structure. CPDs are generated by incomplete carbonization of the polymer clusters utilizing hydrothermal/solvothermal techniques [133]. Interestingly, many carbon-based nanospheres synthesized via hydrothermal/solvothermal methods do not neatly fit into categories like CQDs, GQDs, CNDs and CPDs due to their unique structures. Therefore, they are often broadly classified as CDs. These CDs also have found extensive use as potential agents for regulating the aggregation of amyloid proteins. Here, we have conducted a comprehensive and systematic analysis of how various types of CDs inhibit amyloid aggregation. We compared their efficiency and mechanisms of inhibition, offering valuable insights and research directions for future investigations in this area (see Table 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CPDs and CNDs for inhibiting amyloid aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: CPDs and CNDs have been widely investigated for their capacity to prevent amyloid aggregation and are regarded as promising inhibitors in the field of amyloid-related illnesses. Gao et al. produced nitrogen-doped CPDs by subjecting o-phenylenediamine (oPD) and HNO3 to a one-step hydrothermal procedure [57]. The N-doped CPDs could efficiently control the aggregation of Aβ and remove Aβ mature fibrils in a matter of seconds to minutes (Fig. 5 a). More importantly, their ability to scavenge Aβ plaques was validated in C. elegans CL2006, indicating its potential for the treatment of AD. Due to epigallocatechin gallate's (EGCG) effective inhibition of amyloid aggregation, Lin et al. synthesized a range of CPDs derived from EGCG (E-CPDs) via a hydrothermal method using EGCG and o-phenylenediamine (oPD) [58]. The researchers discovered that the ability of E-CPDs to suppress certain processes and their ability to emit light can be modified by changing the ratio of EGCG to oPD, a dopant containing nitrogen. Moreover, it was discovered that 75E-CPDs, created using 75 mM EGCG and 50 mM oPD, exhibited the highest efficacy in inhibiting and disaggregating Aβ. The 75E-CPDs efficiently stopped the creation of misfolded Aβ, rapidly disintegrated fully formed Aβ fibrils, identified the plaques resulting from Aβ aggregation, reduced Aβ-induced intracellular ROS, and increased the longevity of CL2006 nematodes from 14 days to 20 days. It can be seen that the appropriate proportion of drug molecules derived CDs from drug molecules with therapeutic effects plays a pivotal role in inhibiting amyloid aggregation in the preparation process. Lin's research can be used as a reference for the preparation of nanomedicines by deriving CDs from other related drug molecules. Lin et al. has used a one-step hydrothermal technique to synthesize photosensitizer-doped CPDs (MB-CPDs) [59]. Their research revealed that when exposed to 650 nm NIR light, MB-CPDs effectively generated ROS (Fig. 5b). This procedure successfully impeded the formation of Aβ fibrils and disaggregated existing mature Aβ fibrils, leading to a significant rise in cell viability in the cultivated cells from 50 % to 83 %. MB-CPDs were discovered to increase the longevity of AD nematodes by 4 days in in vivo tests. Importantly, MB-CPDs exhibited significantly stronger inhibitory effects compared to both MB alone and previously studied CPDs.

Wang et al. examined the possible influence of CDs on the aggregation behavior of human islet amyloid polypeptide (hIAPP). They synthesized a range of CDs using small molecules, including CPDs (also namely PDs-1 and PDs-2) and CNDs, for this purpose [60]. The research discovered that the PDs and CNDs successfully hindered the process of hIAPP fibrillation, whereas the GQDs encouraged the creation of hIAPP fibrils. Furthermore, PDs-1, CNDs, and CQDs were shown to be very potent inhibitors of hIAPP fibrillation. Furthermore, PDs and GQDs exhibited distinct methods in mitigating hIAPP-induced INS-1 cell death. This study highlighted that the inhibitory effects of the CDs are strongly linked to their structural and surface characteristics. The structure of the CDs and the characteristics of the conjugated ligands on their surface determines the active mechanism. The nontoxic PDs (PDs-1, PDs-2) and GQDs are very effective at shielding INS-1 cells from the toxicity of hIAPP, despite the fact that the GQDs have the opposite impact on hIAPP fibrillation as the other four types of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other types of CDs for inhibiting amyloid aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: Because of their distinct structures, some CDs-based nanomaterials cannot be strictly classified as CQDs, GQDs, CNDs and CPDs, and they are often inexactly classified into CDs. These CDs also have been widely used as candidates for regulating the aggregation of amyloid. Malishev et al. used l-lysine or d-lysine to create enantiomeric CDs (L-Lys-C-dots and D-Lys-C-dots) based on the structural properties of CDs. The researchers found that the chirality of CDs has a substantial impact on the aggregation process of Aβ42, the form of fibrils, their cytotoxicity, and their interactions with membranes [52]. Their study determined that L-Lys-C-dots had a greater attraction to Aβ42 monomers and fibrils compared to D-Lys-C-dots. As a result, they effectively control the aggregation process of Aβ42, the contact with membranes, and the harmful effects on cells. Interestingly, chiral cyclodextrins produced from aspartic acid (L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs) were synthesized utilizing L/d-aspartic acid using a fast microwave-assisted single-step process [61]. The findings indicated that D-Asp-CDs had a stronger impact in enhancing amyloid fibrillation in comparison to L-Asp-CDs. Subsequently, researchers conducted further investigations into the interaction between amyloid and L/D-Asp-CDs and determined that the quenching process was of a static nature. Furthermore, the interaction between amyloid and D-Asp-CDs resulted in a more stable complex compared to the interaction between amyloid and L-Asp-CDs. This difference in stability accounts for the bigger impact of D-Asp-CDs in inducing amyloid fibrillation compared to L-Asp-CDs. The experiments clearly reveal that the surface chiralized-CDs have a major influence on amyloid fibrillation. Transmissible spongiform encephalopathies are a collection of disorders characterized by the aberrant folding and clustering of prion proteins. Arad et al. discovered a group of chiral cyclodextrins (CDs) that were manufactured using either D- or l-lysine (Lys) in order to suppress amyloid aggregation [62]. Their findings demonstrated that the interaction between chiral cyclodextrins (CDs) and the central aggregation portion of prion peptides (PrP, 106–126 sequences) in the presence of lipid bilayers exhibited a strong preference for a certain stereoisomer. In addition, L-Lys-CDs demonstrated superior efficacy compared to D-Lys-CDs in controlling the structural alteration and clustering of PrP. In conclusion, alterations in the conformational conformation of the material surface, such as CDs with chiral structures, are critically important in inhibiting amyloid aggregation, in addition to the chemical functional groups on the surface of CDs. Additionally, this serves as a crucial point of reference for the development of nanomedicines to treat disorders linked to amyloid.

Bloch et al. showed that tyrosine-derived cyclodextrins (CDs) synthesized using tyrosine and citric acid may effectively prevent the formation of fibrils by hIAPP, reduce the harmful effects of hIAPP on cells, and hinder the peptide's capacity to rupture cell membranes [51]. Furthermore, a comprehensive set of spectroscopic, microscopic, and thermodynamic tests, coupled with cell viability analysis, confirmed that the tyrosine-derived CDs successfully replicated the fibril inhibition characteristics of natural polyphenols. In addition, the researchers discovered that the CDs demonstrated significant disruption of fibrils, surpassing the performance of CDs made using phenylalanine. The findings may be mostly attributed to the differentiation between the CDs that contain phenol residues and those that include benzene groups, as shown by the structural analysis. The hydrogen bonding interactions within the phenol residues are crucial for the efficacy of the tyrosine-derived CDs. Yang et al. discovered that an increased fraction of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of oxidized CDs resulted in a greater inhibition of Human Insulin fibrillation [63].

Wu et al. discovered that dopamine-conjugated cyclodextrins (CDs) could prevent the aggregation of Human calcitonin (hCT), which is a hormone peptide that plays a critical role in calcium-phosphorus metabolism and is released by parafollicular cells. These CDs are particularly effective during the fibril growth phase and are also capable of dissociate preformed hCT amyloids [64].

Roger M. Leblanc et al. reported on a type of biocompatible and BBB permeable CDs prepared by carbon powder [43]. The inhibitory potential of CDs on Aβ42 and Aβ40 fibrillations was confirmed using ThT test, CD spectrum analysis, and AFM imaging. The molecular dynamics simulations demonstrated that the hydrophilic surface of CDs could impede Aβ fibrillation. In addition, the CDs were shown to be successful in suppressing the activity of β-secretase 1 (BACE1) and slowing down the creation of Aβ42 hazardous substances in a laboratory setting. Through the modification of transferrin on the surface of the CDs, researchers achieved effective delivery and penetration of the blood-brain barrier in a zebrafish model. Later, Zhou et al. created amphiphilic yellow-emissive CDs (Y-CDs) using an approach that included ultrasonication [50]. The Y-CDs revealed the capability to traverse the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and had exceptional capabilities as nanocarriers. In addition, the researchers discovered that Y-CDs may reduce the expression and release of Aβ by suppressing the synthesis of amyloid precursor protein (APP) in laboratory experiments. Y-CDs have a hydrophilic surface yet display greater hydrophobic properties compared to hydrophilic CDs, owing to their amphiphilicity. The hydrophilic surface has the ability to exclusively connect with Aβ, resulting in the production of longer structures. This interaction increases the flexibility of Aβ, which is advantageous in preventing the formation of Aβ fibrils.

Luo et al. recently introduced a new kind of Resveratrol-derivatized CDs. These CDs were created using a synthetic mixture of ternary deep eutectic solvent, specifically referred to as Res-DES-CDs [65]. The Res-DES-CDs that were created exhibited a fluorescence probing ability that is activated when in contact with amyloid fibrils. Additionally, they effectively removed reactive oxygen species (ROS) while causing no harm to cells and being readily taken up by them. Luo et al. discovered that Res-DES-CDs exhibited a prompt response to fibrils without causing any disruption to the process of aggregation. Res-DES-CDs show potential as useful probes for real-time monitoring of amyloid aggregation. In addition, Res-DES-CDs could efficiently remove reactive oxygen species (ROS) caused by amyloids. Meanwhile, Li et al. developed and synthesized Nile red (NR)-based CDs (NR-CDs) for the purpose of tracking the aggregation of amyloids [66]. The researchers discovered that NR-CDs had a strong attraction to α-synuclein fibrils and showed a significant increase in red fluorescence. However, they did not display any response to the monomer form of α-synuclein (Fig. 5c). Ultimately, the NR-CDs were used as a fluorescent probe that activates upon detecting and seeing α-synuclein fibrils in vitro and in vivo. Additionally, they were used to track the progression of amyloid aggregation.

From the review above, the physicochemical properties of surface on CDs play a decisive role in the inhibition of amyloid aggregation. For instance, different chirality of the CDs leads to varying inhibitory effects on amyloid aggregation, and CDs derived from natural drug molecules that inhibit amyloid aggregation often produce other unexpected therapeutic effects in addition to their original effects. The results of these studies indicate that CDs could serve as therapeutic agent and ﬂuorescence probes for neurodegenerative and other conditions involving amyloid aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-based composite nanomaterials for inhibiting amyloid aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: CDs have been used as inhibitors, carriers, and other functional units due to their exceptional physicochemical and biocompatibility qualities. Consequently, CDs combined with other components have been widely employed to develop novel composite inhibitors for inhibiting amyloid aggregation. As compound inhibitors, CDs not only function as carriers, but also provide various adjuvant therapeutic effects. For example, Kuang et al. prepared core-shell nanocomposite (namely Fe3O4@CDs) using CDs and Fe3O4 nanomaterial [67]. Curcumin (CUR) has been chosen for its capacity to shield cells from Aβ-induced neurotoxicity by hindering Aβ aggregation. Nevertheless, CUR is vulnerable to photodegradation and may also deteriorate in the absence of light. The cellular absorption efficiency and bioavailability of CUR are significantly low, mainly because to its limited solubility in water and stability in aquatic environments. Therefore, Fe3O4@CDs nanocomposites were used to load CUR. The authors discovered that CUR-Fe3O4@CDs had a significant attraction towards Aβ42 and has the capability to efficiently hinder Aβ fibrillation. Furthermore, studies on cellular activity have shown that CUR-Fe3O4@CDs could suppress the harmful effects of Aβ42 fibrils on PC12 cells. In addition, these nanocomposites are also efficient in decreasing the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) caused by Aβ42 fibrils in PC12 cells. Our research first suggested an innovative therapeutic approach for Alzheimer's disease, focusing on both the aggregation of Aβ proteins and the infection caused by Candida albicans. In this research, we prepared a high-performance nanoassembly system (gCDs-E) using glucose-derived CDs and epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG). The findings demonstrated that gCDs-E exhibited dual functionality by both inhibiting the formation of Aβ fibrils and disassembling existing Aβ fibrils, while also efficiently suppressing the activity of Candida albicans. Experimental results have also indicated that the toxicity induced by amyloid aggregations could be suppressed by gCDs-E. Moreover, in vivo experiments have shown that gCDs-E are capable of crossing the BBB, clearing Aβ deposition, and ameliorating memory impairments in APP/PS1 transgenic AD model mice [46]. Wang et al. created a multi-target nanocomposite to prevent the formation and progression of AD by blocking the aggregation of amyloid and removing reactive oxygen species (ROS) [54]. The nanocomposites, known as HSA-BFP@CDs, are created by combining triple-functionalized human serum albumin (HSA-BFP) with CDs. HSA-BFP acts as a theragnostic agent that targets Aβ, while CDs work as a ROS scavenger. The authors discovered that HSA-BFP@CDs has the capacity to detect Aβ plaques by a fluorescence "off-on" effect at 700 nm when it interacts with Aβ aggregates. Furthermore, HSA-BFP@CDs has the capability to efficiently hinder the aggregation of Aβ and eliminate various reactive oxygen species (ROS), comparable to the other nanocomposites stated before. In vivo experiments confirmed that HSA-BFP@CDs can effectively reduce and inhibit amyloid plaques while also clearing the oxidative stress. Additionally, HSA-BFP@CDs can also effectively diagnose AD by detecting reductions in Aβ deposition and ROS levels. Our group has successfully created a sort of versatile nanoassemblies, known as yCDs-Ce6, by the combination of photosensitizers (chlorin e6) with yellow fluorescent CDs. When compared to other inhibitors, yCDs-Ce6 shown the capacity to block the aggregation of Aβ for a duration of 7 days, disassemble existing Aβ aggregates, decrease the toxicity caused by Aβ, and hinder microbiological development by both photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT). The synthesized yCDs-Ce6 shown a notable capacity to selectively target Aβ aggregates, visually designate Aβ aggregates, traverse the blood-brain barrier under near-infrared light exposure, and eliminate amyloid deposition in APP/PS1 mice using photodynamic therapy and photothermal therapy [68].

There has been significant advancement in recent years in the study of using CDs-based inhibitors for the diagnosis of amyloid-related diseases. For instance, Bu et al. designed a novel dual-mode biosensor by modifying the surface of an electrode with CDs and electrodepositing polyaniline (PANI) film. This design forms a Z-scheme heterojunction, reverses the photocurrent signal, followed by the attachment of the recognition peptide of Aβ oligomers on the electrode surface, ultimately achieving ultra-sensitive detection of these oligomers [69]. The researchers discovered that Aβ oligomers may be used to create a biosensor of the sandwich-type. This biosensor demonstrated a much-improved cathodic photocurrent response and induced a change in the intensity of electrochemiluminescence at wavelengths of 555 nm and 640 nm. The photoelectrochemical detection range of oligomers was determined to be from 10 fM to 0.1 μM, with a detection limit of 4.27 fM. In addition, the electrochemiluminescence approach produced a detection range that extended from 10 fM to 10 nM, with a detection limit of 6.41 fM.

GQDs are a subset of graphene or graphene oxide, whose structure is entirely derived from and dependent upon graphene [134,135]. Zero-dimensional sp2 carbon nanomaterials, or GQDs, have garnered a lot of interest in biological applications because of their distinctive mechanical, electrical, and structural characteristics [98]. While the effectiveness of GQDs in preventing amyloid aggregation has been well-documented, there remains a lack of evaluations concerning the mechanisms through which GQDs inhibit amyloid formation. GQDs may impede the beta-amyloid protein's (Aβ) aggregation by means of hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions [136]. According to Liu et al., a decrease in negative surface charge amplifies the inhibitory impact of GQDs. These interactions between the surface charge and the GQDs are crucial for the aggregation of Aβ protein [21]. Ghaeidamini et al. examined the distinct ways by which graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets and graphene quantum dots (GQD) prevent the development of amyloid fibrils from α-synuclein (α-syn) [28]. They discovered that graphene oxide sheets lowered alpha-synuclein aggregation rate largely by encapsulating monomers, limiting primary nucleation and elongation. In contrast, GQDs have less interactions with monomers and fibrils. The researchers utilized a variety of biophysical techniques to examine the kinetic properties and effectiveness of these inhibition effects, including thioflavin T (ThT) fluorescence assays, circular dichroism spectroscopy (CD), dynamic light scattering (DLS), fluorescence spectroscopy, and atomic force microscopy (AFM). Computer simulations and in vitro studies are routinely used to create synergies to identify patterns of pharmaceutical interactions. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation is a computational chemistry technique that mimics the motion of atoms and molecules to study the physical characteristics of matter [137]. For instance, MD simulations can forecast how biomaterials will change in conformation in various settings and how they will connect to other biomolecules [138]. Kim et al. employed molecular dynamics simulations (MD) to analyze the binding mode of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to α-syn fibrils. This study was aimed at better elucidating the mechanism of interaction between GQDs and α-synuclein (α-syn) fibrils and understanding how GQDs leads α-syn fibrils to depolymerize [27]. GQDs attach themselves immediately to the α-syn N-terminal cross-beta region. Based on NMR spectroscopy evidence about the chemical shift of α-syn, the initial binding between GQDs and α-syn is mainly influenced by the charge interaction between the positively charged region of α-syn and the negatively charged carboxyl groups of GQDs. After 50 ns, the outer monomer of α-syn experiences a complete rupture of its β-sheet structure. This leads to the liberation of the C-terminal segment from the central region, which subsequently engages with the opposing surface of GQDs. At a period of 50 ns, the β-sheet portion of the outer monomer begins to diminish, as shown by the time-dependent secondary structure map generated by the Protein Secondary Structure Dictionary (DSSP) method (Fig. 6 b). This result suggests that the interaction between GQDs and the α-syn fibrils causes a significant breakdown of the fibril structure.

Islet Amyloid Polypeptide (IAPP) and Human Islet Amyloid Polypeptide (hIAPP) are proteins that are linked to type Ⅱ diabetes. IAPP, also known as Islet Amyloid Polypeptide, is a hormone produced by the beta cells of the pancreatic islets. It is found in the same secretory granules as insulin [139]. IAPP is essential for controlling the development of islets, the growth of islet β cells, and the release of glucagon [140]. IAPP may produce amyloid fibrils throughout the development of type Ⅱ diabetes, which deposit in islets and cause islet β cell malfunction and death [141]. The accumulation of amyloid is a characteristic feature of type Ⅱ diabetes and is strongly linked to the development of the disease. The human counterpart of islet amyloid polypeptide, sometimes referred to as IAPP, is denoted as hIAPP. Hormone Amylin (hIAPP) is likewise released by pancreatic islet β cells and operates in a similar manner as IAPP, playing a substantial role in the pathogenesis of type Ⅱ diabetes. Irregular clumping and the development of fibrous structures of hIAPP may cause dysfunction and death of islet β cells, potentially affecting the generation of insulin and the control of blood sugar levels [142]. Wang et al. found that GQDs bind to IAPPs by several interactions, such as hydrogen bonding, hydrophobicity, and electrostatics. These interactions reduce the β-sheet concentration of IAPPs and prevents aggregation [25]. The effects were identified in vitro using the thioflavin T (ThT) kinetic test, fluorescence quenching, circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy, cell activity assay, and discrete molecular dynamics (DMD) simulation (Fig. 6a). The zebrafish embryo model was selected to validate the effects in vivo. Microsecond all-atom molecular dynamics simulation is a computational biology approach that mimics the motions of atoms and molecules to study the behavior and dynamics of large biological molecules, such proteins and nucleic acids, over periods of one millionth of a second. In contrast to discrete molecular dynamics (DMD), this approach provides more accurate insights into both the structure and energy.

Through microsecond scale all-atom molecular dynamics modeling, Zhu et al. discovered that charged GQDs can disrupt the structural stability of hIAPP fibrils, diminish the β-sheet content, and interfere with the stability of the hydrophobic core and hydrogen bond formation at the fibrils interface [70]. Positive and negative GQDs and hIAPP fibrils exhibit diverse binding sites, dynamics, and interactions. Electrostatic interactions are predominant and are supported by π-π stacking, salt bridges, and hydrogen bond interactions. In terms of destructive effect, binding methods, and binding strength, negative GQDs has been proposed as a more effective amyloid inhibitor than positive GQDs [70].

Targeting the amyloid fibrils in the Staphylococcus aureus biofilm, Wang et al. investigated the possibility of using GQDs to prevent the development and structure of bacterial biofilms [71]. Using confocal microscopy, circular dichroism spectroscopy (CD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and molecular dynamics modeling, the effects of GQDs on Staphylococcus aureus biofilm were examined. Molecular dynamics simulations reveal that bacterial amyloid protein (epenol-soluble modulins) interacts with GQDs at the carboxyl group edge near the N-terminal. This interaction results in a change in the protein's shape. Methionine and lysine, two positively charged residues in the PSMs protein molecule, electrostatically engage with the negatively charged carboxyl group at the edge of GQDs (Fig. 6e). This interaction facilitates the formation of a stable complex from GQDs and PSMs, leading to modifications of the secondary structure of PSMs. PSMs self-assemble to create amyloid fibrils with a characteristic beta-sheet when GQDs are not present. GQDs, on the other hand, cause a decrease in the quantity of α-helix and β-sheet structures in PSMs molecules and an increase in β-turn. Because of this structural alteration, GQDs can successfully prevent PSM fibrils synthesis, which is essential for the stability of Staphylococcus aureus biofilms. In addition, GQDs can modify the PSMs' self-assembly process, potentially compromising the structural integrity of the biofilm and causing its dispersion. This finding offers a crucial theoretical foundation for the development of new antimicrobial tactics, particularly in the hunt for cutting-edge drugs that might prevent the growth of bacterial biofilms. Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a leading neurodegenerative condition, the pathophysiological mechanisms of which are not fully understood. Amyloid plaque buildup is associated to the neurodegenerative alterations observed in AD. Through its contact with the cell membrane, Aβ causes a range of downstream processes that result in neuroinflammation, tau disease, neuronal death etc [143]. Tang et al. investigated how Aβ in Alzheimer's disease (AD) accumulates and interacts with cell membranes in relation to graphene quantum dots [72]. An investigation was conducted to assess the effect of GQDs on the fluidity of the neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cell membrane using confocal fluorescence microscopy. Using discrete molecular dynamics (DMD) modeling, the interaction mechanisms of GQDs with Aβ monomer (Aβ-m), oligomer (Aβ-o), and fibrils (Aβ-f) were examined (Fig. 6c). Simulation results suggest that the interaction of GQDs with Aβ can reduce its ability to form ordered structures. This is particularly evident with Aβ-oligomers, where GQDs can inhibit the formation of β-sheet structures, thereby preventing Aβ aggregation (Fig. 6d). The strong interaction between graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the Aβ peptide is mainly attributed to their amphiphilic nature, which enables various types of interactions. These interactions include hydrogen bonding between the polar residues of the Aβ monomers and the epoxide, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups on the GQDs, aromatic stacking, and electrostatic attraction between the positively charged residues of the Aβ monomers and the carboxylic acid groups on the GQDs. The findings indicate that GQDs can successfully disrupt the Aβ membrane axis and avert subsequent unfavorable occurrences. This offers fresh perspectives into potential treatments for Alzheimer's disease.

Li et al. produced minuscule CQDs by Pulsed Laser Ablation (PLA) and examined their capacity to diminish neurotoxicity and impede the aggregation of Aβ42 [45]. Nair et al. verified the fluorescence quenching effect between CDs and silver nanoparticles using a Finite Difference Time Domain (FDTD) simulation [73]. These simulations have improved our understanding of the interactions between CDs and silver nanoparticles and their influence on the fluorescence quenching effect. Designing sensors based on Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) to identify the various phases of beta-amyloid fibrosis requires an understanding of this knowledge. Pandya and colleagues investigated the relationship between neurodegenerative disorders like AD and the formation of Aβ [74]. Using molecular dynamics simulations and docking investigations, researchers examined the interactions between carbon-based nanoparticles with various surface chemical characteristics, such as fullerenes and fullerenols, and Aβ (Fig. 6f). According to docking data, in 68 % of Aβ conformations, fullerenes and fullerenols are coupled with the peptide N-terminal region. The hydrophobic intermediate region of Aβ contains the high affinity binding site of fullerenes (E = −48.31 kJ/mol), whereas the charged Aβ-N terminal region is the preferred interaction site for fullerenols, with a binding energy of −50.42 kJ/mol. Because fullerenes and fullerenols have different surface chemistries, there is a variation in binding. Furthermore, Aβ′s N-terminal and intermediate regions are crucial for Aβ aggregation. Thus, fullerenes and fullerenols can collectively prevent Aβ from aggregating, which has significant implications for the development of nanoparticles targeting Aβ-related disorders.

Biomaterials development has significantly advanced thanks to simulation and computational technologies, these tools has enabled researchers to predict and optimize material properties before laboratory testing, saving both time and money, and accelerating the creation and application of new materials [144]. Research on the simulation computation of carbon materials is currently quite prevalent, whereas studies focusing on the computational analysis of CDs are less common. Future research will focus on the complex structure of CDs due to their modeling challenges and diverse mechanisms of action. The current study is primarily focused on how amyloid proteins, especially Aβ peptides, interact with nanomaterials (such as CQDs, GQDs, fullerenes, and fullerenols) and how these interactions affect the processes of amyloid aggregation and fibrosis (see Table 5). These findings could significantly impact our understanding and treatment of neurodegenerative illnesses, like Alzheimer's disease, which are caused by amyloid buildup.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photosensitizing CDs for modulation of amyloid aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: Phototherapy may be categorized into two main types: Photothermal therapy (PTT) and Photodynamic treatment (PDT). PDT specifically involves the administration of photosensitizing materials followed by targeted light irradiation at specific disease lesions [145]. PDT is based on the photodynamic effect, a photosensitization response with the participation of aerobic molecules [146,147]. Due to its ability to manage space and time and its low invasiveness, PDT has attracted considerable interest for the treatment of localized illnesses such as malignancies, microbial infections, and other ailments [148,149]. On the other hand, photothermal therapy (PTT), which is another kind of light-based medical treatment, uses targeted heat creation to treat a specific location of concern [150–152]. Light-assisted therapy, in comparison to surgical treatment or chemotherapy, provides a therapeutic method that reduces harm to the adjacent tissues.

Recently, PTT and PDT for research of the amyloid aggregation disease have been receiving extensive attention. Research has shown that the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by photodynamic therapy (PDT) may effectively impede the formation of amyloid aggregates and decrease the harmful effects caused by amyloid [153]. The primary cause of blocking amyloid is mostly due to the oxidation of amino acid residues on the amyloid [154]. For instance, when subjected to PDT, Aβ can decrease the likelihood of self-assembly by inhibiting the conversion of amyloid protein from its random coil structure to β-sheet-rich aggregates. During the first phases of Aβ self-assembly, aggregates develop a structure consisting of two β-strands (Leu17-Ala21 and Ile31-Val36) that are connected by a turn. This structure is created by hydrophobic contact. Nevertheless, the process of photo-oxidation may diminish the hydrophobic nature and reduce the structural stability of Aβ by modifying the polarity of Met35. Furthermore, the process of photo-oxidation of Aβ peptide will result in the loss of its inherent capacity to engage in reactive oxygen species generation and redox processes. The Aβ peptide undergoes oxidation of its Met, His, and Tyr residues owing to the oxidative stress present in its surroundings. Specifically, the amino acid residue Met35 in Aβ can undergo redox reactions and is crucial in causing cytotoxicity generated by Aβ. Nevertheless, if the Aβ peptide is oxidized prematurely by reactive oxygen species, the oxygen atoms replace the sulfur electron pairs in the Met35 amino acid residues, resulting in the formation of sulfoxide or sulfone. Moreover, the three histidine (His) residues of Aβ play a role in coordinating the transition metal ion with its imidazole ring. In this case, the premature oxidation of the ROS produced by catalysis center formed with metal ion and Met will be significantly reduced. Simultaneously, photo-oxidation further weakens the interactions between peptides.

Studies have shown that the temperature rise caused by PTT significantly affects the hydrogen bonds among amyloid proteins. Higher temperatures have a tendency to disrupt these hydrogen bonds [155]. Furthermore, while hydrophobic contact is an essential need for Aβ aggregation, the force may also be altered by temperature. Elevated temperatures have the potential to disturb the interactions between molecules, leading to the breakdown of aggregates [156,157]. CDs with PTT and PDT functionalities have the benefits of precision, simplicity of handling, high efficiency, and low adverse effects as revolutionary therapeutic methods. Under the irradiation of light at a specific wavelength, CDs can produce ROS and local heating, which oxidize amino acid residues and disrupt the interaction between amyloids. Here, we summarized recent research progress in the use of the light-responsive properties of CDs to intervene in amyloid aggregation diseases.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) have both beneficial and harmful effects. Oxidative stress is responsible for several illnesses in the body. However, it may also be used as a therapeutic approach to cure ailments [158].

Based on photodynamic therapy, researchers have proposed several photo-responsive CDs-based nanomaterials. These CDs-based photo-responsive nanomaterials can generate local ROS upon light irradiation, thereby enabling site-specific modulation of amyloid aggregation or amyloid fibril disruption [159] (see Table 6). Chung et al. used ammonium citrate as the primary substance to produce CQDs using a one-step hydrothermal technique. They then chose branched polyethylenimine (bPEI) to protect the surface of the CQDs [75]. bPEI@CDs have hydrophilic and cationic surface characteristics, enabling it to engage with negatively charged residues of Aβ and impede Aβ aggregation. Under irradiation, bPEI@CDs generate ROS, which not only exhibit stronger effects on monomer inhibition but also promote amyloid fibril dissociation (Fig. 7 a). Consequently, the amino acid residues of Aβ protein undergo conformational changes and lose their toxicity mediated by ROS. Subsequently, Chung et al. used o-phenylenediamine to prepare multifunctional CDs (namely OPCDs). The presence of nitrogen-containing polyaromatic compounds on the surface of OPCDs decreases the production of oxidative stress and the propensity of Aβ to aggregate, which is facilitated by Cu(II) [76].
