Light irradiation significantly intensified the inhibitory impact of OPCDs by generating 1O2 that oxidizes crucial Aβ residues, namely His and Met. Multifunctional OPCDs have the ability to eliminate Cu(II) from Aβ-Cu(II) complexes and also to oxidize the active site of Aβ via photodynamic processes. This simultaneous and cooperative action effectively interrupts the process of Cu(II)-mediated Aβ aggregation and decreases the harmful effects of the Aβ-Cu(II) aggregation process. Based on the previous work, Chung et al. designed an Aβ-targeting, red-light responsive CDs (Apta@CDs) to inhibit Aβ aggregation [77]. Apta@CDs also exhibit good Aβ targeting in complex neurophysiological Settings. Apta@CDs, when exposed to red light, modulate Aβ aggregation by oxidizing Aβ residues. The application of Apta@CDs to the brain of 5xFAD mice further validated the ability of Apta@CDs to resist amyloid aggregation in vivo, demonstrating excellent preventive and therapeutic ability (Fig. 7b). Chen et al. proposed the preparation of carbon dot-based covalent organic framework. It was discovered that CDs based covalent organic frameworks (COFs) possess excellent ROS generation ability and good biocompatibility [78]. Li et al. used Ce6 and PEI as precursors to design phosphorescence CDs, which emit near-infrared luminescence for up to 2 h. These CDs demonstrate good tissue penetration and high signal-to-noise ratio (98.4), making them suitable for deep tissue and long-term continuous luminescence imaging in vivo [79]. In addition to their remarkable tissue penetration ability, CDs produce substantial amounts of ROS under illumination. These characteristics underscore the great potential of CDs as a multifunctional nanoplatform for biomedical applications. Further, our group has designed a multifunctional CDs based nanomaterial (yCDs-Ce6). This nanomaterial shows the capability to inhibit the formation of amyloid aggregates for a duration of 7 days. Additionally, it was able to disassemble existing aggregates, diminish the harmful effects caused by amyloid aggregation, and limit the development of microorganisms via the use of PDT and PTT (Fig. 7c) [68]. Furthermore, yCDs-Ce6 has a high degree of specificity in targeting amyloid aggregates and can effectively see and identify these aggregates. yCDs-Ce6 can pass across the blood-brain barrier when exposed to near-infrared light, and it can effectively remove amyloid deposits in APP/PS1 mice by PDT and PTT (Fig. 7d). Sun group has produced photosensitizer-doped CPDs (MB-CPDs) using a hydrothermal technique in a single step [59]. Upon exposure to 650 nm NIR light, the study found that MB-CPDs, present at a concentration of 2 μg mL−1, produced 1O2. This process significantly hindered the formation of Aβ fibrils and disintegrated pre-existing mature Aβ fibrils (Fig. 7e). The vitality of the grown cells was enhanced from 50 % to 83 % by this procedure. Subsequent in vivo experiments provided additional evidence that MB-CPDs extended the lifetime of worms with AD by a duration of 4 days. The inhibitory capacity of MB-CPDs is much greater than that of MB and exceeds the effectiveness of previously documented CPDs (Fig. 7f). On the other hand, our group synthesized CNDs with photocatalytic properties using o-phenylenediamine [80]. Upon exposure to 580 nm light irradiation, CNDs catalyze the production of singlet oxygen from O2 and singlet oxygen irreversibly oxidizes amyloid.

Additionally, hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks (HOFs), gold nanoclusters, and small molecules such as indocyanine green have been reported for their application in photodynamic therapy for Alzheimer's disease [160–163]. The photodynamic effects of carbon dots have also been used in cancer treatment [164]. The primary challenge with photodynamic therapy lies in its inadequate targeting, which can result in the destruction of normal nerve cells during treatment. This critical issue is currently the focus of ongoing research endeavors. ROS is a two-edged sword. The photodynamic therapy shows that carbon dots exhibit photocatalytic properties, which are due to their massive, conjugated structures. To reduce ROS damage to normal tissues, photodynamic carbon dots must be targeted more precisely. Carbon dots eliminate reactive oxygen species in the absence of laser while producing reactive oxygen species in the presence of laser, which may be a topic of future study.

Heat has the potential to disturb homeostasis; as amyloid production is temperature sensitive, local temperature fluctuations can influence Aβ aggregation [165]. As local temperatures increase, the hydrogen bonds between amyloid proteins are disrupted. Concurrently, variations in temperature also influence the intermolecular contact forces such as hydrophobic interaction and π-π stacking [166].

Lee et al. developed nitrogen (N)-doped CNDs (N-CNDs) with robust absorption in the near-infrared spectrum, superior photostability, and exceptional biodegradability [81]. Optimized N-CNDs have potential not only as a novel photoacoustic (PA) imaging agent but also as a highly efficient photothermal treatment (PTT) agent in living organisms, owing to their robust optical absorption at a particular wavelength. After N-CNDs accumulated at the tumor location, laser irradiation was used to elevate the temperature to 50 °C. In order to counteract the Aβ aggregation resulting from an imbalance in Cu2+ homeostasis, Ye et al. created nitrogen-doped CQDs and constructed a nano-system called CQD-RAW, which included encapsulating the CQDs inside a macrophage membrane (RAW-M) [53]. CQD-RAW could efficiently collect excessive Cu2+ and prevent the fast aggregation of Aβ. Furthermore, the excellent photothermal characteristics of CQD-RAW may also effectively disintegrate the fibril precipitates using photothermal therapy (PTT). Additionally, near-infrared imagers recorded brain temperature before and after illumination. The brain temperature differential between the CQD-RAW + NIR group and the PBS control group rose with time, reaching 11.14 ± 0.87 °C after 4 h after material administration. In vitro and in vivo studies further confirmed that the CQD-RAW effectively crossed the BBB by photothermal effect, reducing Aβ deposition, mitigating neuroinﬂammation, and improving memory deficits in APP/PS1 mice. Our group has designed multifunctional nanoassemblies (yCDs-Ce6) from chlorine e6 and CDs [68]. Compared with other previously reported inhibitors, yCDs-Ce6 showed superior performance. Specifically, yCDs-Ce6 effectively suppresses amyloid aggregation, disaggregates existing aggregates, and reduces amyloid aggregation-induced cytotoxicity by PDT and PTT. Furthermore, yCDs-Ce6 shows the capacity to traverse the blood-brain barrier via photothermal effects and effectively eliminate amyloid accumulation in APP/PS1 mice using PDT and PTT. Notably, a considerable temperature shift of 5.1 °C was observed after injecting yCDsCe6 and receiving 808 nm laser irradiation. Masha et al. conducted research that presents a cost-effective and environmentally friendly approach to manufacturing carbon-based quantum dots (CBQDs) utilizing citric acid and formamide. The paper also includes an analysis of the photothermal properties of these CBQDs [82]. The CBDQs have a compact, spherical shape and possess a high degree of crystallinity, with an average particle diameter of 4 nm. The produced colloidal quantum dots (CBQDs) had notable absorption characteristics, emitting light in the red spectrum, and displaying exceptional ability to disperse in water. Additionally, the photothermal research provided further confirmation that the CBQDs had excellent heat generation capabilities, thereby demonstrating their significant potential for future photothermal applications. Liu et al. performed a study on the photothermal properties of CDs produced from urea and citric acid using the solvothermal technique in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) at different temperatures [83]. The CDs synthesized at 150 °C (150-CDs) had a photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) of 61.3 %, which is much better than the PCE of the CDs synthesized at 180 °C (180-CDs). Authors concluded that the photothermal effect of the CDs was caused by the lattice vibration of their carbonized cores. The presence of considerable carbonization and a high concentration of surface carbonyl groups (CO) notably increased the absorption band in the deep red region, resulting in improved photothermal performance of the CDs. We made light-sensitive carbon nanodots L-CNDs using solvent-thermal methods. L-CNDs produce singlet oxygen under 630 nm irradiation, demonstrating exceptional photocatalytic capabilities. L-CNDs exhibit 68.25 % photothermal conversion efficiency at 808 nm, enabling targeted heating and Aβ aggregation control [159].

The scientific usefulness and research potential of CDs-based nanomaterials were thoroughly shown using the therapeutic platform of PPT and PTT. Furthermore, the use of CDs for amyloid disorders has also shown significant growth. To minimize the amount of damage done to normal tissue, it is essential to determine how to reach the objective of the lesion site. Modifications may be made to nanomaterials to target pathogenic factors. The wavelength of the laser, as well as the intensity, irradiation duration and location, are also highly crucial factors that should be taken into consideration. In addition, the concentration of the nanomaterials also needs to be considered. In summary, the use of CDs-based PPT and PDT has introduced a new and innovative therapeutic approach for studying disorders connected to amyloid.

The misfolding, aggregation, and deposition of amyloid in vivo is thought to be an important marker and causative factor in numerous neurodegenerative diseases and metabolic disorders. Over the past decades, tremendous eﬀort has been made to block misfolding, aggregation, and deposition of amyloid through nanomaterials and nanotechnology, to eventually cease the progress of these disorders. Among various nanomaterials, CDs have shown great potential in the application research of amyloid aggregation diseases, leading to major breakthroughs.

So far, many studies have reported the application of CDs in amyloid-related diseases. Despite CDs exhibiting structural similarities, there are still great differences in their chemical and physical structures. These variations lead to different CDs classifications that are substantial in determining the specific application and functionalities in the diverse scientific areas [167]. Based on the above characteristics, these types of CDs have both similarities and differences in inhibiting amyloid aggregation. The study of the influence of CDs with varying structures on the inhibition of amyloid aggregation-related diseases will significantly contribute to the preemptive design of CDs, enhancing their therapeutic potential in treating such conditions.

Currently, only a limited number of studies systematically categorize the application of different types of CDs in amyloid-related diseases. By comprehensively reviewing the effects and inhibitions of different types of CDs on the amyloid aggregation process, researchers can identify clear research directions for future related research. Such an approach will facilitate the efficient selection of CDs-related nano inhibitors and the design of more effective nanomedicines. Carbon dots are a novel form of nanomaterial that has exceptional biocompatibility and does not exhibit any harmful properties [168]. On the other hand, its cytotoxicity may be influenced by a wide variety of parameters, such as its size, dispersity, surface modification, precursor selection, and other factors. During future research, it is essential to conduct more investigations into the mechanisms that underlie the effect of these parameters on carbon dots cytotoxicity and to ensure the safety of its widespread application.

Several CDs have undergone clinical studies to assess their efficacy in both in vitro and in vivo settings using animal models. However, none of these CDs have been brought to the market for commercial use. In the future, it is necessary to focus on exploring and researching CDs that can be truly clinically translated. The function of CDs remains underutilized, especially the promising functions of the removal of ROS, phototherapy, and fluorescence imaging. Fully harnessing these functions in the context of amyloid aggregation diseases also represents a crucial direction that necessitates future research endeavors to ensure their applicability and effectiveness in clinical settings.
